article.title: ASML Looks Beyond Its EUV Monopoly, Plotting Advanced Packaging Tools to Capture the Next Decade of AI Chip Manufacturing
article.summary: ASML is expanding beyond EUV lithography into advanced chip packaging, shipping its first dedicated scanner and researching bonding tools for AI processors.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

ASML Holding, the Dutch company that holds a monopoly on the extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography machines essential to manufacturing the world's most advanced semiconductors, is charting a course into new territory. In a Reuters exclusive published on March 2, the company's Chief Technology Officer Marco Pieters disclosed that ASML is researching tools for advanced chip packaging and bonding, areas that sit downstream from its traditional lithography stronghold, as reported by [Reuters](https://reuters.com/technology/asml-plots-future-chipmaking-tools-ai-beyond-euv-2026-03-02/).

The move signals that ASML, valued at roughly $560 billion, sees the AI-driven transformation of chip architecture as an opportunity to diversify well beyond the light-based patterning systems that made it indispensable to every leading-edge foundry on Earth.

## What We Know

Pieters, who was promoted to CTO in October 2024 after replacing Martin van den Brink, told [Reuters](https://reuters.com/technology/asml-plots-future-chipmaking-tools-ai-beyond-euv-2026-03-02/) that the company is planning on a 10-to-15-year horizon. "We look, not just for the next five years, we look at the next 10, maybe 15 years," he said, adding that ASML is examining "what are potential directions the industry could take, and what would it require in terms of packaging, bonding, etc."

The strategic rationale is rooted in a fundamental shift in chip design. Until recently, processors from companies like Nvidia and AMD were essentially flat, single-die devices limited to roughly the size of a postage stamp. Increasingly, the most powerful AI chips are built as vertical stacks or horizontal fusions of multiple specialized dies, connected through nanometer-scale links. This "skyscraper" architecture allows designers to increase computational throughput beyond what a single die can deliver, but it demands extreme precision in the packaging and bonding steps that assemble these multi-chip modules.

ASML has already shipped its first product targeting this market. The [TWINSCAN XT:260](https://asml.com/en/products/duv-lithography-systems/twinscan-xt-260), an i-line (365 nm) scanner purpose-built for advanced 3D packaging, was delivered in late 2025. According to [ASML](https://asml.com/en/products/duv-lithography-systems/twinscan-xt-260), the system processes up to 270 wafers per hour, roughly four times the throughput of existing advanced packaging lithography tools. It handles wafers up to 1.7 mm thick, more than double the standard 775 micrometers, and supports warped substrates and through-silicon alignment for via and deep-trench patterns. The XT:260 targets technologies like TSMC's Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrate (CoWoS) and Intel's Foveros, which are the dominant platforms for assembling high-bandwidth memory stacks and multi-chiplet AI accelerators, as reported by [Tom's Hardware](https://tomshardware.com/tech-industry/semiconductors/asml-launches-revolutionary-lithography-scanner-for-advanced-3d-packaging-twinscan-xt-360-machine-quadruples-throughput).

Pieters told Reuters that ASML is "researching to what extent we can participate" in packaging and bonding, and that "accuracy is becoming more and more important" in these processes. He also said ASML's engineers are investigating whether chips can be manufactured beyond current size limits, and that AI will be integrated into the company's control software and inspection systems as its tools become faster.

## What We Don't Know

ASML has not disclosed specific revenue targets or financial projections for its advanced packaging initiative. The company has not announced a timeline for delivering tools beyond the XT:260, and much of the work Pieters described remains in research phases. Equipment qualification at major foundries involves lengthy validation cycles, making it difficult to project when packaging tools could contribute meaningfully to ASML's top line.

It is also unclear how ASML's packaging ambitions will position it against incumbents in the backend equipment market, such as Applied Materials and Tokyo Electron, which already supply bonding, deposition, and etching tools used in advanced packaging lines.

## Analysis

The packaging bottleneck is real and growing. TSMC is scaling its CoWoS capacity from roughly 35,000 wafers per month in late 2024 to a projected 130,000 per month by the end of 2026, yet Nvidia has already locked in approximately 60 percent of that output. Alphabet reportedly reduced its 2026 tensor processing unit production targets because Nvidia's priority allocations left insufficient CoWoS capacity available.

For ASML, the opportunity is twofold. First, it can leverage its deep expertise in precision optics and wafer handling, which are directly relevant to the lithographic steps within advanced packaging workflows. The XT:260 already demonstrates this, bringing scanner-grade overlay correction to a domain previously served by older stepper tools. Second, as Pieters noted, the packaging expansion will "co-exist" with ASML's existing EUV business rather than replace it, according to [The Decoder](https://the-decoder.com/asml-plans-to-expand-beyond-chip-lithography-into-advanced-packaging/).

But ASML is entering a market with established competitors and a different margin profile than its EUV monopoly, where a single high-NA machine sells for more than $350 million. Packaging equipment is a higher-volume, lower-price-point business. Whether ASML can command premium pricing in this segment, or whether it will need to compete on throughput and cost, will shape whether the diversification proves financially material over the next decade.

With its stock trading at roughly 40 times forward earnings and a market capitalization near $560 billion, ASML is betting that its lithography heritage gives it an edge in the increasingly precision-critical world of 3D chip assembly. The next several years will determine whether that bet pays off.
